author: @TheOldGuyPhil
text:
Love how the  white plastic is  turning the color of carbon. :) Check this   DOUBLE capacitance from University of Washington inexpensive new method for manufacturing supercapacitor electrodes. https://www.techbriefs.com/component/content/article/tb/techbriefs/manufacturing-prototyping/28309# 
Aerogels made from formaldehyde and other carbon bases are dried/heated preserving their high 3D surface area. Thin sheets of molybdenum disulfide or tungsten disulfide (Used in industrial lubricants) are broken using sound waves and mixed into the gel. Combined with adhesives and “carbon-rich material” it is rolled into sheets a few thousandths of an inch thick. Discs were cut and assembled into coin cell battery that tested with higher capacitance.
 One gram of aerogel contains about as much surface area as a football field.

author: @Karabetter
text:
Hello Robert, I really appreciated seeing the theory, design, and building of the probe.  This gives us common language for measuring the square resistance and allows comparisons of experimental material designs :) :) :)
However, I have a concern, unless you trim the material being measured to the same standard dimension, it can cause inaccuracy.  Here is how:
Resistors act in parallel, and as you put more material in parallel, the resistance will drop.  Therefore, sheets that are larger than the probe will be putting the material outside the border of the probe in parallel with the material within the borders.  Since the material is typically thin, the measurement won't increase in a linear relationship as the sample gets more and more oversized.  But it's those first few centimeters around the outside that will have the greatest effect.
It's a perfectly good and usable tool, just need to be aware of that effect. :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: There are other faults with this mate and plenty of arguments about design - for example some folks say you should do it in concentric circles to avoid disparity of flow at the ends - but to be honest as a tool for the home researcher i feel it is just fine - but thanks for pointing out the one of the issues

replies.author: @Karabetter
replies.text: SMOG That is correct. However, the material most people are trying to optimize for low resistance will have numbers from ones to maybe hundreds of ohms.  Skin resistance will be tens of thousands of ohms  (unless maybe it's an android doing the tests. lol.)  My point being that unlike for general electronic circuit test, skin resistance in parallel should not change the reading of these materials that much. :)

replies.author: @Karabetter
replies.text:
Oh absolutely Robert, these things have to be affordable.  :)
The way to control the parallel effect I am talking about is to standardize in one's lab on sample size.  So for example, make the square probe to be 10cm x 10cm, always use sample material size 20cm x 20cm, and then try to approximate the placing of the probe near the middle.  This way would be more of comparing apples to apples.
Conversely, trying to cut the material to exactly fit 10 x 10 would be hard, especially to avoid damaging the edge.
Cheers mate, good video! :)

replies.author: @timooster1108
replies.text:
If the dimensions of the resistive layer you're masuring are a lot bigger than your measurement device, you could just multiply the value you've measured with a constant factor. 
This factor could be derived mathematically (Oh no, don't do that!), or you could just measure this factor once by comparing the reading of a trimmed and an untrimmed sheet. 
In most cases, this factor will be independent on the material - just measure it once, for one material, and you know it for every future measurement ;) I guess that this factor could be 1.5 or so.

replies.author: @Karabetter
replies.text:
There you go Timo Oster, that should work very well, especially since the samples should be two dimensional sheets.  I was wondering what would be a good calibration material to arrive at the factor, because aluminum foil, although convenient, is such low resistance it might be hard to measure. ;) Maybe steel/iron sheet or stainless screen would do well for that. Carbon sheet or graphite weave would do even better. :)  Afterward, maintaining a consistent sample size would let you use the factor calculation with any sheet material.
Trying to just use the material you experiment to prepare might be misleading for doing the calculation.  Because that's where test material in a home lab might have problems in being uniform across a surface.
There is nothing wrong with having home lab practices be able to produce consistent results, because that makes for less frustration with results when trying to scale up.

author: @ats89117
text:
When you build EMC shields that need to be press fit into place (e.g. equipment covers), you get it drilled in very quickly that some materials are much better for low contact resistance at low contact pressure, while also being resistant to oxidation and other types of corrosion. The winning materials are always gold and tin. As you point out at the end of the presentation, copper will work, but only with a lot of pressure to get past the inevitable surface oxidation. Would be interesting to know what kind of difference you would get with an ENIG coating or even from tinning the copper electrodes on your square...

And, yes, yes, I know I should test it myself, but that looked like a lot of work, and I am lazy scientist! ;-)
